summarization.a: Multiple line chart showing Gibbs free energy of adsorption (ΔG_ads^80 °C) versus nitrogen coverage for the Ni/N system across several crystallographic facets. Adsorption becomes progressively less favorable with increasing coverage for all facets.
summarization.b: Multiple line chart showing ΔG_ads^80 °C versus nitrogen coverage for the Cu/N system. Adsorption energies are consistently more positive than in Ni/N, indicating weaker nitrogen binding on Cu surfaces.
summarization.c: Multiple line chart showing ΔG_ads^80 °C versus oxygen coverage for the Ni/O system, including labeled surface reconstructions such as (100) p(2×2), (110) (2×1)-MR, and (3×1)-MR. Oxygen adsorption is strongly favorable at low coverage and weakens with increasing coverage.
summarization.d: Multiple line chart showing ΔG_ads^80 °C versus oxygen coverage for the Cu/O system, including pairing-row reconstructions. Adsorption becomes less favorable with increasing oxygen coverage and approaches thermoneutral behavior at high coverage.

a.question: What is the temperature condition for which these adsorption free energies were calculated?
In the Ni/N system, does the Gibbs free energy of adsorption generally become more negative (more stable) as the nitrogen coverage increases?
a.answer: No, the energy generally increases (becomes more positive/less stable) as coverage increases.

c.question: Describe the specific surface reconstructions labeled for the Ni/O system in subfigure (c). Which reconstruction appears to be the most energetically stable at low coverage?
c.answer: Subfigure (c) explicitly labels several surface reconstructions for the Ni/O system. For the (100) facet, it identifies $p(2 \times 2)$ and $c(2 \times 2)$ structures, while for the (110) facet, it highlights $(2 \times 1)-MR$ and $(3 \times 1)-MR$ "missing row" reconstructions. At the lowest coverage shown, the $(110) (2 \times 1)-MR$ reconstruction (marked with a green cross) appears to be the most stable, having the lowest $\Delta G$ value below -2.0 eV.

b.question: Comparing the Ni/N system in (a) and the Cu/N system in (b), which metal system exhibits stronger (more negative) adsorption energies for Nitrogen?
